title: Mobile and Ubiquitous Games
informalDescription: This course covers the design and development of mobile games, going beyond basic apps to include mixed reality and location-based experiences. It's a great choice if you're interested in how virtual games can interact with the real world.

title: Game Design
informalDescription: You'll look at the bigger picture of game design here, including psychology, storytelling, and market trends. It's less about coding and more about learning to think like a designer to create truly engaging player experiences.

title: Intermediate Programming
informalDescription: This is where you'll really sharpen your coding skills with more complex language features. Heads up: the projects can be pretty time-consuming, but they're essential for learning how to build high-quality software.

title: Programming in C/C++ as a Second Language
informalDescription: If you're already solid with programming, this class will get you up to speed with C and C++, especially core concepts like pointers. There's a lot of hands-on coding with an autograder, but you'll likely need to do some self-study outside of class for the best results.

title: Programming in Java as a Second Language
informalDescription: This is a quick way to pick up Java if you already know how to code. The labs and participation quizzes are generally straightforward, so it's a manageable path to an A if you stay on top of the assignments.

title: Data Structure Implementation and Analysis
informalDescription: You'll get hands-on experience implementing core data structures and algorithms. The assignments are practical and often provide pseudocode, making it more about solid implementation than deep theoretical deep dives.

title: Introductory Computer Organization
informalDescription: This class gives you a ground-up look at how computer systems are designed and operated. It's a heavy workload with a lot of labs, projects, and exams, so definitely be prepared to put in the time.

title: Principles in System Design
informalDescription: You'll explore the basics of operating systems, compilers, and networking. It can be a bit of a challenge, so it's a good idea to start assignments early and make use of the help lab hours.

title: Boolean Logic and Discrete Structures
informalDescription: This course covers boolean logic and structures like automata. It's a fair amount of work, and you'll find yourself relying on the textbook quite a bit. Studying with friends can really help you stay on track here.

title: Discrete Mathematics for Computer Science
informalDescription: You'll learn the mathematical foundations of computer science, specifically focusing on how to abstract and solve computational problems using discrete math.

title: Computational Linear Algebra
informalDescription: A deep dive into linear algebra concepts like matrices, vector spaces, and eigenvalues, all within a computational context.

title: Advanced Programming and Problem Solving with C++
informalDescription: This class focuses on advanced language concepts for building complex, high-performance software. It has a reputation for being challenging, so be ready for some serious problem-solving.

title: Digital Image Processing
informalDescription: You'll learn the fundamentals of signal and image processing, which are super useful for fields like graphics, AI, and game development.

title: Computer Graphics
informalDescription: This course covers the principles of 3D graphics, including modeling, illumination, and rasterization. It's reported to be a bit challenging, but rewarding if you're interested in visual computing.

title: Computer Game Development
informalDescription: A great introduction to building both 2D and 3D interactive games. Students generally have a positive experience in this class.

title: Projects in Advanced 3D Computer Graphics
informalDescription: This is a project-based course where you'll work on advanced graphics topics like animation and geometric modeling.

title: Computer Simulation
informalDescription: You'll learn about different types of simulation (event-driven, continuous, etc.) and the probability concepts needed to analyze them.

title: Computational Photography and Vision
informalDescription: A tough but interesting class focusing on computer vision and photography. Projects are in Python/Numpy and include things like panorama stitching and face warping. Heads up: the lectures might feel a bit separate from the homeworks, so office hours will be your friend.

title: Project in Computer Vision
informalDescription: In this course, you'll actually build a computer vision system, covering topics like robot navigation, human motion tracking, and 3D modeling from photos. It's moderately demanding and student feedback can vary, so be ready to dive into the technical details.

title: Introduction to Virtual Reality
informalDescription: You'll explore VR systems and software through both midterms and practical projects. It's a challenging course, but most students find it really rewarding.

title: Information Retrieval
informalDescription: This class covers how search engines work, including indexing and clustering text. The projects (like building a web crawler and search engine) are in Python and can be tough if you're working solo, but they're incredibly cool to see in action.

title: Introduction to Data Management
informalDescription: SQL is a huge part of the industry, and this course gives you plenty of practice with it. It can feel a bit dry at times, and the logic behind queries can be tricky, but it's very useful for any developer.

title: Project in Databases and Web Applications
informalDescription: This is a heavy-duty project class where you'll build a full-stack Java application from scratch. It's infamously time-consuming—especially the first few projects—but it gives you great experience with things like XML parsers and Docker deployments.

title: Principles of Data Management
informalDescription: This is widely considered one of the toughest implementation classes. You'll need to be very comfortable with C and ready for some deep technical work, but it's highly regarded by students.

title: Beyond SQL Data Management
informalDescription: A great follow-up to 122A that explores NoSQL databases. It's generally well-received and focuses on projects and practical applications of modern data tech.

title: Next Generation Search Systems
informalDescription: Focused on building modern search systems, this course centers around a quarter-long project. It's moderately demanding and can feel a bit like a mobile dev class depending on the quarter.

title: Introduction to Security and Privacy
informalDescription: Considered a great intro to the security mindset. You'll work on projects like buffer attacks and web security using Python, JS, and C. There's a final exam, but overall it's a manageable and highly recommended course.

title: Parallel and Distributed Computing
informalDescription: You'll study parallel systems through a series of heavy projects, labs, and exams. It's moderately demanding with a significant workload, so plan accordingly.

title: Computer Networks
informalDescription: This course covers network architectures and protocols. It tends to follow an older textbook, so you might find yourself supplementing with online resources. It's a heavy workload, so be prepared for a lot of homework and exams.

title: Advanced Computer Networks
informalDescription: A practical course where you'll manually configure routers on the CLI. It's very specialized for those interested in network engineering, and results in a lot of hands-on experience with routing protocols.

title: Computer and Network Security
informalDescription: This class is a deep, mostly theoretical dive into cryptography and protocol security. It's math-heavy and exam-focused with no coding projects, but it's perfect if you want to understand the 'under the hood' math of security protocols like SSL/TLS.

title: Internet Applications Engineering
informalDescription: A study of web-focused engineering, covering concepts like HTTP, REST, and Peer-to-Peer systems. Reviews are a bit scarce for this one, so it's a good idea to chat with the instructor or peers about what to expect.

title: Concepts in Programming Languages
informalDescription: This class explores the structure and notation of various programming languages, including paradigms like logic, functional, and object-oriented programming. Heads up: the course structure can feel a bit disorganized depending on the professor, so be ready for constant deadlines and a heavy project load.

title: Compilers and Interpreters
informalDescription: In this course, you'll actually build your own compiler in Java! It's a 5-stage project that's generally very well-regarded by students for its clear structure and interesting concepts.

title: Project in Compilers and Interpreters
informalDescription: Basically 142A on steroids. It's a much more independent, grad-level style course with less guidance. Only take this if you're really passionate about compiler architecture and ready to work on your own.

title: Principles of Operating Systems
informalDescription: Focuses on OS resource management. Depending on the professor, there might be an optional Pintos project that’s great for learning C but requires a lot of independent work. The difficulty can vary, so check who's teaching.

title: Project in Operating System Organization
informalDescription: You'll design parts of an OS like memory managers. You can often choose your language (including Python), which makes it manageable, but you might get more out of it if you challenge yourself with C.

title: High-performance Computers and Program Optimization
informalDescription: Explores how computer architecture affects program optimization, covering topics like RISC, Superscalar, and VLIW architectures. There aren't many reviews for this one, so checking the prerequisites carefully is a must.

title: Embedded Software
informalDescription: A fun breadboard-focused class where you'll use basic C to work with hardware components. The hardware can be a little finicky, but overall it's a great intro to embedded systems.

title: Programming in Multitasking Operating Systems
informalDescription: Covers user and systems-level programming, including a fair bit of shell scripting. It's an alright course, just be aware it's often taught by a single professor.

title: Internet of Things (IoT) Software and Systems
informalDescription: Very similar to CS 145 but focused on IoT. You'll use Arduino libraries for things like sensors and Bluetooth. It's generally an easy A and you get out what you put into your final project.

title: Digital Logic Design
informalDescription: This is very low-level, focusing on things like circuit design and boolean algebra. If you really liked ICS 51, you might enjoy this, though some students find the teaching style a bit hit-or-miss.

title: Computer Architecture
informalDescription: You'll study the components of a computer like the ALU and control units. It's a moderately demanding course with a focus on practical projects and exams.

title: Computer Design Laboratory
informalDescription: Explores computer instruction sets. It's reported to be moderately demanding with mixed feedback, so it helps to be interested in the low-level side of things.

title: Design and Analysis of Algorithms
informalDescription: Covers essential techniques like divide-and-conquer and dynamic programming. It's a heavy workload with lots of homework and exams, but absolutely foundational for any CS major.

title: Formal Languages and Automata
informalDescription: A theory-heavy course focused on automata and complexity (think P vs NP). It's proof-based, which isn't for everyone, but it gives you a fascinating look at what computers can actually solve.

title: Graph Algorithms
informalDescription: You'll study algorithms for graph theory problems. Be prepared for an exam-heavy course that might focus more on memorizing algorithm details than practical application.

title: Computational Geometry
informalDescription: Focuses on algorithms for geometry and modeling. It's a solid choice if you like algorithms and want to see how they're applied to vision and graphics.

title: Project in Algorithms and Data Structures
informalDescription: A project-based algorithms class. You'll implement things like sorting and graph networks. It's generally manageable and involves writing reports on the data you collect.

title: Quantum Computation and Information
informalDescription: Learns about models for quantum computing based on quantum mechanics. It's reported to be quite challenging and project-heavy, so be ready for a deep dive.

title: Introduction to Applied Cryptography
informalDescription: An extension of the number theory from 6D applied to encryption. There's a decent amount of proof writing, and the quality can vary depending on who's teaching.

title: Network Optimization
informalDescription: Uses modeling techniques for large-scale integer programming, with applications in communication, transportation, and logistics networks. Reviews are scarce, so definitely check the prerequisites before signing up.

title: Introduction to Optimization
informalDescription: A broad, math-heavy introduction to optimization with a lot of linear algebra. It's consistently reported as a challenging course with a heavy workload.

title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
informalDescription: A broad intro to AI that reviews a lot of 6B and Stats 67 material. It's a manageable class with a final project (like a card game AI), though some find the lectures a bit dry.

title: Neural Networks and Deep Learning
informalDescription: Explores deep learning theory and exercises. It depends a lot on the TAs, as the lectures can be very theoretical. Be prepared for a final group project and mandatory attendance.

title: Artificial Intelligence Frontiers: Technical, Ethical, and Societal
informalDescription: Covers the cutting edge of AI and the underlying ethical, legal, and societal challenges they create. Reviews are scarce, so talking to peers or the instructor is a good move here.

title: Bioinformatics
informalDescription: Introduces biology problems that lend themselves to computational and algorithmic analysis. Another one with scarce reviews, so double-check the prerequisites.

title: Project in Artificial Intelligence
informalDescription: A project-focused AI course where you might build an AI for something like Minecraft. It's well-received and focuses heavily on practical development.

title: Applications of Probability in Computer Science
informalDescription: You'll apply probability to real-world CS problems like algorithm analysis, network traffic modeling, and reliability. It's a heavy workload of projects and exams, moderately demanding, and student feedback is often mixed.

title: Machine Learning and Data-Mining
informalDescription: A theory-focused dive into ML principles. You'll need solid linear algebra and stats here. Projects usually involve experimenting with different classifiers on real-world datasets.

title: Algorithms for Probabilistic and Deterministic Graphical Models
informalDescription: You'll learn about graphical models for knowledge representation through a heavy series of projects and exams. Feedback is mixed, so be ready for a challenge.

title: Project in Computer Science
informalDescription: Focuses on solving a real-world problem using your CS knowledge. Reviews are scarce, so definitely ask around for expectations.

title: Project in Computer Science
informalDescription: A continuation or alternative for solving substantial real-world problems. Like 180A, detailed reviews are hard to find, so check with the department.

title: Introduction to Computational Biology
informalDescription: This course uses theories and methods from CS and physics for biomolecular modeling and analyzing biological structures. It's generally reported as a challenging course with mixed student feedback.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Biology and Medicine
informalDescription: Covers computational methods in molecular biology. It's moderately demanding with a mix of projects, labs, and exams.

title: Probabilistic Modeling of Biological Data
informalDescription: A focus on Bayesian frameworks for modeling biological data, including things like Markov models and gene finding. Reviews are scarce, so check the syllabus and prerequisites carefully.

title: Computational Systems Biology
informalDescription: Inference and modeling of gene regulation, signal transduction pathways, and cellular processes. Not many reviews here, so it's best to ask an advisor or previous students.

title: Project in Bioinformatics
informalDescription: A team-based course where you'll design and test solutions to bioinformatics problems. Be aware that student feedback has been pretty mixed to negative lately.

title: Special Topics in Information and Computer Science
informalDescription: Explores unique special topics in ICS. Expect a project-heavy focus with some homework and practical assignments.

title: Individual Study
informalDescription: Independent research or 1-on-1 study with a faculty member. It's very flexible and centers around a practical project of your choice.
